name: Gideon
description: Gideon, also known as Jerubbaal, was a judge of Israel who lived during a time of oppression by the Midianites. Born into the tribe of Manasseh, Gideon was a humble farmer when an angel of the Lord appeared to him and tasked him with leading the Israelites to victory. Initially doubting his abilities, Gideon requested signs from God to confirm his calling, including the famous fleece test. He courageously destroyed his father's altar to Baal, earning the name Jerubbaal, meaning "Let Baal contend with him." Gideon assembled an army of 32,000 men, but God instructed him to reduce the number to 300, emphasizing that the victory would be due to divine intervention, not military strength. With trumpets, empty jars, and torches concealed within the jars, Gideon and his small band of warriors launched a surprise attack on the Midianite camp. The confusion and panic caused by the noise and light led the Midianites to turn on each other, resulting in a decisive victory for the Israelites. Gideon refused to be made king, believing that God alone should rule over Israel. He requested gold earrings from the spoils of war, which he used to create an ephod that later became an object of idolatry, a tragic flaw in his otherwise exemplary leadership. Gideon's story is a testament to faith, courage, and the power of God to use ordinary individuals to accomplish extraordinary things. He is remembered as a pivotal figure in Jewish history, liberating Israel from oppression and demonstrating the importance of relying on divine guidance.
subtitle: Judge and Liberator of Israel
achievements: Defeated the Midianites with a small army
Led Israel to 40 years of peace
Inspired faith and courage in the Israelites

keyMyths.title: The Angel's Visit
keyMyths.description: An angel of the Lord appears to Gideon while he is threshing wheat in a winepress, commissioning him to save Israel from the Midianites. Gideon initially doubts his ability but eventually accepts the divine calling after receiving signs.

keyMyths.title: The Fleece Test
keyMyths.description: Gideon tests God's will by placing a fleece on the threshing floor, asking for dew to be only on the fleece and then only on the ground. These signs confirm his mission.

keyMyths.title: The Battle with 300 Men
keyMyths.description: God reduces Gideon's army from 32,000 to 300 men, emphasizing that the victory will be due to divine intervention. The 300 defeat the Midianites with trumpets, jars, and torches.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The primary biblical source for the story of Gideon.
sources.text: The primary biblical source for the story of Gideon.

sources.author: Josephus
sources.description: A historical account of Gideon and the Israelites.
sources.text: A historical account of Gideon and the Israelites.

associations.name: Fleece
associations.significance: Represents Gideon's testing of God's will and the confirmation of his divine mission through miraculous signs.

associations.name: Trumpet
associations.significance: Used to instill fear and confusion in the Midianite army, symbolizing the power of faith over military might.

associations.name: Empty Pitchers
associations.significance: Concealed lamps within pitchers, symbolizing the hidden power and light of God's presence revealed at the opportune moment.

associations.name: Three Hundred Men
associations.significance: Represents the small but faithful force Gideon led, demonstrating that victory comes from God, not sheer numbers.

associations.name: Jerubbaal
associations.significance: Meaning 'Let Baal contend against him,' this name signifies Gideon's opposition to idolatry and his commitment to monotheism.

associations.name: Barley Loaf
associations.significance: In a dream, this symbolized the destruction Gideon would bring to Midian, showcasing divine premonition.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Heracles and Gideon are tasked by a divine power to overcome seemingly insurmountable odds and deliver their people from oppression.
Similar to Gideon, Cú Chulainn is a divinely favored warrior who uses cunning and bravery to defeat larger, more powerful enemies.
Both Gideon and Thor are champions of their people, relying on divine assistance and unconventional tactics to achieve victory over their foes.

worshipPractices.name: Rejection of Baal Worship
worshipPractices.description: Gideon's destruction of the altar of Baal and the Asherah pole signifies a return to the exclusive worship of Yahweh, emphasizing monotheism over polytheism.

worshipPractices.name: Ephod Incident
worshipPractices.description: Gideon creates an ephod from the spoils of war, which becomes an object of idolatry for the Israelites, highlighting the challenges of maintaining religious purity even after victory.

cultural.modernLegacy: Gideon's story is often cited as an example of courage, faith, and leadership in the face of adversity, inspiring individuals and communities to overcome challenges through reliance on divine guidance.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Divine Calling
extendedContent.content: Gideon was born into the tribe of Manasseh and lived in the town of Ophrah. He came from a humble background, working as a farmer when the angel of the Lord appeared to him. During this time, the Israelites were heavily oppressed by the Midianites, who raided their crops and livestock, leaving them in poverty and fear. The angel's appearance marked a turning point in Gideon's life, as he was chosen to lead the Israelites to freedom. Initially, Gideon doubted his abilities and questioned why he, of all people, was selected for such a monumental task. He sought reassurance from God, requesting signs to confirm his calling. These signs included the famous fleece test, where Gideon asked God to make the fleece wet with dew while the ground remained dry, and then vice versa. These miraculous events convinced Gideon of his divine mission and prepared him to take on the challenge of leading the Israelites against the Midianites.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Military Strategies
extendedContent.content: Gideon's most renowned quest was leading a small Israelite force against the vast Midianite army. Recognizing that a large army might lead the Israelites to believe their victory was due to their own strength, God instructed Gideon to reduce his army significantly. Through a series of tests, Gideon whittled down his forces to just 300 men. Armed with trumpets, empty pitchers, and torches, Gideon and his men launched a surprise attack on the Midianite camp at night. The Israelites shattered the pitchers, revealing the torches, and blew their trumpets, creating a deafening noise and causing chaos among the Midianites. The Midianites, disoriented and terrified, turned on each other, and the Israelites pursued and defeated them. This victory secured Israel's freedom from Midianite oppression and established Gideon as a national hero. Gideon's clever tactics and unwavering faith played a crucial role in achieving this remarkable triumph.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Impact
extendedContent.content: Gideon's legacy extends beyond his military achievements. After the victory over the Midianites, the Israelites offered to make Gideon their king, but he refused, stating that the Lord should be their ruler. This act demonstrated his humility and commitment to theocratic rule. However, Gideon's later actions were not without controversy. He created an ephod from the spoils of war, which became an object of idolatry for the Israelites, leading them astray from the exclusive worship of Yahweh. Despite this misstep, Gideon is remembered as a judge who delivered Israel from oppression and upheld the worship of God. His story serves as a reminder of the importance of faith, courage, and humility in leadership. Gideon's life illustrates both the potential for greatness and the challenges of maintaining righteousness in the face of power and temptation. His narrative continues to inspire and resonate with audiences today.

shortDescription: Gideon, a judge of Israel, led a small army to victory over the Midianites, guided by divine signs and unconventional tactics. His story highlights faith and courage.
